data_quality: v0.1 best-effort population from open vendor catalogs (SparkFun, Adafruit, Arduino official) and hands-on community datasheets. Each entity's compat claims trace to source_ids on its attributes. Verification pass against vendor pages required before production launch; this dataset is sized to prove the configurator engine end-to-end on a second vertical, not for general consumer trust.

sources.name: SparkFun open product catalog
sources.scope: Used as a citation for products SparkFun stocks. SparkFun's catalog is open and the vendor explicitly encourages outbound traffic; pricing and stock are the vendor's authoritative figures.

sources.name: Adafruit open product catalog
sources.scope: Used as a citation for Adafruit-manufactured boards (Feather/QT Py/Metro) and breakouts that Adafruit maintains official Arduino/CircuitPython libraries for.

sources.name: Arduino official product database
sources.scope: Authoritative source for first-party Arduino boards (UNO, Mega, Nano, MKR, Portenta) — MCU chip, voltage, pin count, official MSRP.

attributes.note: Classic 5V learning board. Pairs trivially with the entire 5V sensor catalogue but cannot drive 3.3V-only modules without a level shifter on the input side. Single hardware UART means SoftwareSerial when adding GPS/GSM alongside USB-Serial debug.
Drop-in UNO R3 replacement on a modern 32-bit Renesas core. 5V logic is preserved so legacy shields work, but the MCU is internally 3.3V — VCC pin still says 5V to the shield world.
WiFi/BLE comes from a co-processor ESP32-S3 talking over an internal serial bridge. Use the WiFiS3 library, not raw ESP-IDF — application code still runs on the Renesas RA4M1.
Picked when GPIO count or 4 hardware UARTs (RAMPS 3D-printer boards, big LED matrix projects) matter. Slow 8-bit core — don't expect it to drive WS2812 strips longer than ~500 pixels at 60 fps.
Breadboard-friendly UNO-equivalent. The on-board 3.3V regulator is rated for ~50 mA only; do not power an ESP-01 from it, brown-outs will reset the Nano.
3.3V GPIO — do NOT feed 5V signals from a UNO directly. Has an onboard u-blox NINA-W102 for WiFi/BLE so you can ship a connected project on a single PCB.
Cortex-M4F + 256 KB SRAM is the smallest official Arduino that comfortably runs TensorFlow Lite Micro. 3.3V logic only.
MKR form factor is built around a JST PH 3.7V LiPo connector and integrated charger — pick this when battery operation is non-negotiable. Strict 3.3V logic.
MKR pinout with onboard SD slot and I2S for audio. No wireless — pair with an RFM95 LoRa module if you want range.
Industrial-grade dual-core (M7 + M4) board with USB-C, MIPI camera + display lanes via the high-density connectors. Overkill for blink — picked for vision/audio ML or PLC replacement work.
The reference 30-pin ESP32 dev board most tutorials assume. 3.3V logic only — feeding any 5V sensor signal into a GPIO will damage the chip over time.
S3 adds vector-DSP instructions for ML and native USB without a UART bridge. Picked for camera/audio ML projects on a budget. 3.3V logic.
Cheapest credible WiFi+BLE board. Single-core RISC-V, only 11 usable GPIO. Ships under many brand names; quality control varies — buy from a vendor with returns.
Has an OV2640 camera onboard but NO USB — needs a separate FTDI-style USB-TTL adapter to flash. Famous for poor 5V regulation; power from a clean 5V/2A supply, not the dev USB rail.
$4 dual-core Cortex-M0+ with PIO state machines for bit-banged protocols (DVI, WS2812, SDIO). 3.3V logic. Arduino IDE support is via the third-party arduino-pico core, not Arduino's own.
Pico + Infineon CYW43439 WiFi/BLE on the same board. Use the on-board LED via the WiFi chip's GPIO, not GP25 — common gotcha porting Pico code to Pico W.
RP2350 Cortex-M33 with 520 KB SRAM, dual-core (M33 + RISC-V Hazard3 selectable). Same pinout as the original Pico — most shields and Pico boards work unmodified.
Pico 2 with the same CYW43439 wireless module as Pico W. Best price/perf ratio in 2026 for a small WiFi-capable board.
Postage-stamp size with USB-C and a STEMMA QT (JST SH 1mm) I2C connector — chain Adafruit sensors with no soldering. Pin-compatible with the Seeed Xiao RP2040 footprint.
ESP32-S2 has WiFi but NO Bluetooth — confirm before picking it for a phone-pairing project. Native USB-C HID support is excellent for keyboard/mouse projects.
QT Py footprint with WiFi+BLE+vector DSP. The PSRAM 8 MB variant is the right pick for camera/voice ML; the no-PSRAM SKU is cheaper but limits TensorFlow models to ~200 KB.
Cheapest QT Py and the easiest entry to CircuitPython. No wireless. Great for a USB HID macro pad or a STEMMA-QT sensor logger.
Feather form factor — onboard LiPo charger and JST PH battery jack. Stackable with any FeatherWing. ATWINC1500 WiFi is reliable but slower than ESP32-grade radios.

Cortex-M4F with FPU and 2 MB QSPI flash for CircuitPython library storage. Picked for audio synth, fast DSP, and any project that wants Feather form factor without onboard wireless (pair with an RFM69 or AirLift FeatherWing).
Feather with battery support, USB-C, and an STEMMA QT port. 100 mA LiPo charger — fine for a 1000 mAh cell, slow for 3000 mAh.
RP2040 Feather without WiFi — pair with an AirLift, RFM95, or 4G FeatherWing for connectivity. STEMMA QT, LiPo charging.
UNO footprint but 3.3V logic. Most UNO shields work; any shield that drives a 5V signal into a Metro pin will damage it. Always check shield logic level first.
M4 + onboard ESP32 acting as a WiFi co-processor over SPI. The ESP32 here is *not* programmed as a main MCU — flash AirLift firmware, not Arduino sketches.
UNO clone with USB-C and an onboard Qwiic (JST SH I2C) connector. Drop-in replacement for any UNO R3 sketch.
600 MHz Cortex-M7 — fastest Arduino-compatible MCU on the market. Picked for audio synthesis, fast DSP, and high-channel-count motor control. 3.3V GPIO not 5V tolerant.
Teensy 4.0 + onboard Ethernet PHY pads, SD slot, and PSRAM/flash footprints. Pick 4.1 over 4.0 when you need Ethernet or microSD without a shield.
Feather-format nRF52840 + ESP32 (WiFi co-processor) with first-class Particle Cloud OTA. Worth the markup only if you actually use Particle Cloud — otherwise an ESP32 Feather is half the price.
Realtek RTL8721 dual-band WiFi in Feather form factor. Replaces the older Photon (P0/P1) — sketches written for old Particle Photons need a recompile.
ESP32 in a finished plastic case with a 2.0" capacitive touch screen, speaker, IMU, RTC and 390 mAh LiPo. Picked when you need a demo enclosure on day one and don't want to 3D-print anything.
Pocket-size ESP32 in a finished case with a 1.14" LCD, IMU, IR transmitter, and 200 mAh battery. Grove ports for sensors — Grove cables are 4-pin JST 2.0 mm, not the same as Adafruit STEMMA QT.
Cheapest temp/humidity sensor and the worst quality. Use for learning only — DHT22 is double the price and twice the accuracy. Single-wire protocol; no I2C.
Common upgrade from DHT11. Read interval is once every 2 seconds — sample faster and the library returns NaN. Single-wire protocol with internal pull-up needed (10k to VCC).
Each unit has a unique 64-bit ROM ID — bus several on a single GPIO. Waterproof variant with stainless probe is the standard choice for outdoor / immersion. 4.7 kohm pull-up required.
Barometric pressure + temperature, no humidity. Most breakouts include a 3.3V regulator and level shifters so they are 5V-bus-tolerant despite the 3.3V die.
Full environment sensor (T/H/P) on I2C. Default address is 0x77 on Adafruit, 0x76 on most generic boards — collisions with BMP280 (0x76) are common.
BME280 + a metal-oxide gas sensor. 'IAQ' index requires Bosch's closed-source BSEC library — open-source code only gets you raw resistance, not air quality numbers.
Cheapest 6-DOF IMU but officially EOL — many breakouts are clones. New designs should prefer MPU-9250 (9-DOF) or BNO055 (sensor fusion onboard).
9-DOF (gyro + accel + magnetometer) on a single chip. Magnetometer (AK8963) shares the I2C bus through an internal master and needs special init — don't expect it to 'just work' from the I2C scanner.
Onboard ARM core runs the sensor fusion — outputs absolute orientation as Euler angles or quaternions, no host math needed. Costs more but skips the I2 nightmare of calibrating MPU-9250 by hand.
Echo pin outputs 5V — a 3.3V board (ESP32, Pico, Feather M0) needs a level shifter or a voltage divider on echo. The HC-SR04P variant is 3-5V and is the right pick for 3.3V projects.
Laser ToF (940 nm IR), works in the dark unlike ultrasonic. Multiple sensors share I2C address 0x29 — to use more than one on the same bus, toggle XSHUT pins and remap addresses at boot.
4-meter range vs 2-meter for the L0X. Same 0x29 default address quirk applies. Higher idle current (~20 mA) — not great on coin-cell.
Same I2C address as VL53L0X/L1X (0x29) — never put both on one bus. Has a built-in white LED for consistent illumination; pull-down to disable when reading ambient.
Analog electret mic with auto-gain — better for unknown sound levels than fixed-gain MAX4466. Output is biased at VCC/2; subtract DC offset in software for FFT.

attributes.range: see SparkFun example for HR algo
attributes.note: Reports raw red+IR samples — heart-rate and SpO2 are calculated by the host. SparkFun's algorithm code is GPL; check licensing if you redistribute firmware.
Sees through plastic enclosures unlike PIR — useful for hidden-presence projects. Not FCC-tested; not legal for commercial sale in the US/EU without an antenna-tuned re-cert.
Cheap, reliable, but just a relative reading — for actual lux use a TSL2591 or BH1750. Pair with a 10k pull-down resistor.
Heater pulls ~150 mA at 5V — too much for an ESP32 USB rail without external power. Needs a 24-hour burn-in to stabilize before any calibration is meaningful.
Requires a duty-cycled heater (5V/1.4V at 60s/90s) to read CO correctly. Wiring it to a fixed 5V will return junk numbers — don't ship a CO alarm built on it.
Marketed as 'air quality' but it's a single MOX sensor that responds to many gases at once. Not a CO2 sensor. For CO2 use SCD41 or SCD30.
Higher-quality MOX gas sensor than MQ-series but only available as bare TO-5 — most projects buy the SparkFun breakout. Heater is 5V/40 mA.
Capacitive type doesn't corrode like the resistive ones. Calibrate dry/wet at install — every soil mix has a different curve. PCB coating fails after ~6 months in soil; expect to replace yearly.
0.1 ohm shunt onboard — burden voltage caps usable current at 3.2 A. For higher current (motors, batteries) use INA226 with an external shunt.
External shunt resistor — pick one to set the current range yourself (typical 10 mohm for 8 A). Same I2C address space as INA219 — collision if both on one bus, change addresses with the A0/A1 pads.
Bit-banged 2-wire protocol (data + clock) — not I2C, not SPI. Pair with a 4-wire load cell (typical 1 kg / 5 kg / 20 kg / 50 kg modules). Tare in software at boot.
9600 baud NMEA over UART. First fix takes 30-60 seconds outdoors; cold-start indoors can fail entirely. The patch antenna's ground plane matters — mount on a metal surface for better fix.
GPS+GLONASS+Galileo+BeiDou concurrent. ~5x faster cold-start than NEO-6M, sub-meter when paired with RTK. Picked for vehicle tracking or any moving project.

True NDIR-class CO2 (photoacoustic). The right answer when 'air quality' actually means CO2 ppm. Auto-calibrates on the assumption it sees outdoor air (~420 ppm) once a week — disable ASC if the device lives in a sealed room.
More accurate than DHT22 and physically smaller. Default address 0x44 collides with HTU21D — pick one or the other on a shared bus.
Output is 3.3V regardless of supply — safe for ESP32/Pico. Two trim pots: sensitivity and re-trigger time. Settles ~30 seconds after power-on; ignore the first reading.
True lux readings 188 microlux to 88,000 lux — works in moonlight to direct sun. Address 0x29 collides with VL53L0X/L1X and TCS34725 — keep these off the same bus or use an I2C multiplexer.
The default tutorial OLED. Default I2C address is 0x3C — collides with another SSD1306. Adafruit and U8g2 both have drivers; U8g2 supports both 128x64 and 128x32 variants.
Looks like an SSD1306 but is NOT — using the SSD1306 driver gives garbled output with a grey 4-pixel offset. Use U8g2 with the SH1106 constructor.

Cheap colour TFT, ~30 fps over hardware SPI on UNO. Adafruit_ST7735 + Adafruit_GFX. Beware: 5V VCC is regulated onboard, but logic pins are 3.3V — needs level-shift on UNO/Mega for clean signals at high SPI speeds.
Resistive touch needs a separate XPT2046 SPI controller chip. Adafruit's breakout includes an SD slot on the same SPI bus — share CS pins carefully.
18-bit colour, runs on hardware SPI but slow on UNO/Nano. Use TFT_eSPI on ESP32 for full 60 fps. UNO shield variants exist that wire as 8-bit parallel — much faster but burns 16 GPIOs.
Black/white e-paper. Refreshes once every ~3 seconds for full-screen redraw. Picked for low-power signage that updates rarely. Don't refresh more than once per minute or the panel ghosts permanently.
Common badge / room-display size. Tri-colour (B/W/red) variant exists at the same SKU footprint — confirm which one you ordered before flashing the driver.
Picked for shelf labels and IoT dashboards. Adafruit FeatherWing form factor exists — drop on a Feather and you get an SD slot too.
Bigger than the 2.9 — full screen refresh ~4 seconds. Frame buffer is 15 KB so an UNO can't hold it; use ESP32, Feather M4, Pico, or stream from external SRAM.
Largest mainstream e-paper. 48 KB frame buffer — needs a board with PSRAM (ESP32-S3 with PSRAM, Teensy 4.x, Portenta) for double-buffering. Refresh ~5 seconds.
Daisy-chainable — 4 modules at 60 fps is fine on Arduino UNO. 5V logic; 3.3V boards work with most modules but signal margin is tight, level-shift if you see flicker.
I2C backpack uses a PCF8574 expander at 0x27 or 0x3F. Native HD44780 needs 5V — most 'I2C LCD' boards do work at 3.3V signal but the contrast pot needs adjustment for low-VCC operation.

Each LED draws up to 60 mA at full white — 24 LEDs full-white = 1.4 A from the 5V rail. A 3.3V board signal can drive WS2812B if the first LED is close (within ~30 cm) and the 5V rail is clean; otherwise add a 74AHCT125 level shifter.
Full white draws 18 A — needs a separate 5V/20A PSU, not USB. Inject 5V every 1 m to avoid voltage droop at the strip end.
Direct-drive 5x7 needs 12 GPIOs and Charlieplexing for brightness — most projects pair it with a MAX7219 or shift register instead. Listed for retro builds.
Cheapest hobby servo — 0.65 A stall. Don't power from a UNO 5V pin (max ~500 mA) or you'll brown-out the MCU. Use a separate 5V/2A supply and tie grounds.
11 kg/cm at 6V. Stall current can hit 2.5 A — needs a real PSU. Common counterfeits ship with plastic gears despite metal-gear branding.
Standard 3D printer / CNC stepper. 1.8 deg per full step (200 steps/rev). Always pair with a chopper driver (A4988/DRV8825/TMC2208) — direct H-bridge will cook the windings.
5V geared stepper bundled with a ULN2003 driver board. Slow (max ~15 rpm) but tiny torque is fine for a clock movement, blind-rotator, or robot eye. 0.088 deg per step thanks to the 1:64 gear box.
Up to 1/16 microstepping with the MS1/MS2/MS3 jumpers. 2 A peak with a heatsink — without one, derate to 1.2 A. Cheap and noisy compared to TMC2208.
1/32 microstepping, higher voltage than A4988 (good for fast NEMA17 moves). Pin-compatible with A4988 boards but the Vref formula is different — Vref = 1.0 * I_max, not 0.5.
Near-silent stepper driver — picked for any 3D printer, blind/curtain motor, or fume-hood robot where noise matters. UART mode lets you set current/microsteps in software, not by jumpers.
Old-school BJT-based driver — 1.5 V drop per leg. Wastes power as heat; modern designs prefer DRV8871 (TI) or TB6612FNG. Still fine for hobby 6 V DC motors.
43 A peak, 30 A continuous with proper heatsinking. Picked for ebike / cordless power-tool projects. Logic side is 3.3-5V tolerant.
The yellow plastic gearbox motor every 'Arduino car' kit ships. 1:48 reduction, ~200 rpm at 6V no-load. Pair two with an L298N.
5" quadcopter-class brushless. Needs a separate ESC (BLHeli32 / BLHeli_S). Direct PWM from Arduino is 50 Hz hobby pulse — works but most racing ESCs prefer DShot, which an UNO can't generate.
1.1 A at 5V — well above any MCU GPIO. Drive with a logic-level N-MOSFET (e.g. AOI518) and a flyback diode (1N4001) across the coil.

Common irrigation valve. Normally closed — fail-safe for plant-watering. Drive with a mechanical relay or a 12V-tolerant MOSFET; MUST add a flyback diode across the coil or the back-EMF will kill the driver.
75 mA — borderline for direct GPIO drive. Always use a small-signal NPN or N-MOSFET with a flyback diode. Don't drive from a Pico GP pin directly even though the math says it fits.
Active-LOW input — pulling a pin to GND closes the relay, which surprises new users. Each relay coil pulls ~70 mA from 5V, four = 280 mA — Arduino USB rail can't drive all four hot, use external 5V.

Standard JST-PH 2.0 mm connector for Adafruit Feather and Arduino MKR boards. Includes a tiny PCB BMS — overcharge and over-discharge protected, but NOT short-circuit. Don't reverse polarity, the BMS won't save you.
Same connector as 1000 mAh, double capacity. Fits behind most Feather PCBs with double-stick foam. Charge rate cap: most Feather chargers do 100-500 mA — full charge takes ~5 hours.
Big enough for ESP32 always-on projects with deep-sleep duty cycle (months runtime). Too thick to fit behind a Feather; mount externally.
Bare 18650 with NO protection circuit — needs a TP4056 + DW01 protection board, or it will catch fire on overcharge. Picked over LiPo when cycle life and energy density matter (solar / off-grid).
PCB-pin or wire-leaded variants. Pair with TP4056 + DW01 boards to add protection. Don't trust generic holders to hold a button-top cell securely under vibration — solder tabs to a flat-top instead.
$2 LiPo charger with USB-C input. The version with DW01 + dual MOSFET adds protection — buy that one, not the bare TP4056. Charge rate set by an R3 resistor; 1 A by default.
1.2 V dropout — running it from a 3.7V LiPo cell does NOT give a clean 3.3V. Use an MCP1700/MCP1702 (low-dropout) on battery designs.
Ultra-low-dropout (~625 mV) and quiescent of 2 microamps — the right LDO for a coin-cell or 3.7V LiPo project. 250 mA cap; not for ESP32 transmit peaks.
Boost from 1S LiPo (3.7V) to 5V or 9V. Adjust output via the trim pot — turn it BEFORE wiring the load or you might dump 28V into your sensor. Output ripple ~50 mV; not great for analog.
Workhorse adjustable buck. Quiescent current is high (~5 mA) — kills coin-cell projects. Adjust output before connecting load.
Higher switching frequency (1.5 MHz) than LM2596 — smaller inductor, tighter output ripple. Pick this over LM2596 for new designs.
Negotiates PD from any USB-C laptop charger. Set output voltage with a button. Picked for projects needing 12V/3A from a USB-C wall brick — no barrel adapter needed.
5V LDO — used onboard most cheap UNO clones. Drops up to 8V to heat at 800 mA — needs heatsinking or copper pour above ~250 mA.
Standard Arduino UNO power supply. 5.5/2.1 mm centre-positive barrel jack. Not regulated to 5V — the UNO's onboard linear regulator does the conversion (and gets hot above 1A draw).
1S LiPo charger + 5V boost in one PCB. 500 mA out at 5V (peak 1A). Picked for portable projects where you want 5V regardless of the cell's state of charge.
Solder-able prototyping shield with mini breadboard. Stacks onto any UNO-footprint board. Watch the IOREF pin — your shield logic level follows it.
Standard half-size breadboard. Power rails split mid-board on most cheap clones — bridge them with jumpers if you need a continuous rail.
Plated through-holes both sides — essential for SOIC adapters. Solder cleanly with 350 C iron; cheap perfboards lift pads above that.
Drops on any Feather. SSD1306 controller, I2C address 0x3C. Three side buttons map to user GPIOs. Doesn't fit MKR or Nano — Feather pinout only.
PCF8523 RTC + microSD on a single Wing. SD shares SPI with the Feather radio FeatherWings (LoRa, RFM69) — pick CS pins carefully when stacking.
32 NeoPixels at full brightness can pull 1.9 A — way over the Feather USB rail. Power the Wing's 5V pad from an external supply, not USB.
Sub-GHz packet radio (not LoRa). 200 m line-of-sight typical. Solder a wire antenna or you'll get 5 m range. License-free in the US for the 915 MHz ISM band; EU variant uses 433/868 MHz.
Real LoRa modulation — 1-2 km outdoors with a wire antenna, 10+ km with a Yagi. Software is RadioHead or LMIC for The Things Network. Same Wing PCB as RFM69; different chip.
PCA9685 + dual TB6612FNG. 4 DC motors or 2 steppers. Logic level 5V — works with all Feathers. Doesn't free-pass GPIOs through; you can't stack another Wing on top.
Latching relay only draws power during state change — perfect for battery projects. 2A 240VAC contact rating.
MKR-board carrier with screen, IMU, RGBs, 5 capacitive touch pads, 2 relays, and Grove ports. Picked for any 'put together a demo in a day' project on MKR. Doesn't fit UNO/Nano.
PCB clone of the half-size breadboard layout — solder once, never re-wire. Perfect for shipping a one-off project without going to PCB fab.
Slot the Nano in, get screw terminals on every pin. Lifesaver for industrial wiring — but the BLE/IoT variants are 3.3V; don't feed 5V from a relay coil straight in.
Slots a 30-pin ESP32 DevKitC into a screw-terminal carrier with onboard 5V/3V3 regulators and a barrel jack. 38-pin variants don't fit; verify pin count before buying.
PCB that takes 2 Feathers side by side or 1 Feather + 1 Wing in landscape. Lets you mount a battery-charging Feather and a radio Feather without stacking.
Bare RFM69 module — solder a 7.8 cm wire antenna to ANT for full 915 MHz range. NOT 5V tolerant; use only with 3.3V boards or level shifters.
Real LoRa, kilometre range outdoors. Compatible with The Things Network. 915 MHz US, RFM95-868 for EU, RFM96-433 elsewhere — wrong-region modules can be confiscated by the FCC.
Cheapest way to add WiFi to a UNO via AT commands at 9600 baud. CH_PD pin must be HIGH or it sleeps; many tutorials forget this. Hot — pulls 170 mA TX peak.
1 km outdoor with the antenna variant. NOT WiFi — it's Nordic's proprietary 2.4 GHz. Decoupling cap (10 microfarad) right next to the module is mandatory; without it, packets drop randomly.
Bluetooth Classic SPP — pairs as a serial port to Android. iOS rejects HC-05; for iPhone projects use BLE (HM-10 or onboard ESP32 BLE).
Cheap BLE serial bridge. Works with iOS and Android. Many AT firmware versions exist — buy from a vendor that publishes which firmware ships, or you'll fight the AT command set.
2G is shutdown in the US/AU/SG/NZ — do NOT pick this for new US projects. Pulls 2 A peaks during transmit; needs a 4.7V/2A supply with bulk caps, not USB.
LTE Cat 1 — global rollout, including post-2G US. Picked over LTE Cat-M (LTE-M) when you need 10 Mbps for camera streams. Same 2 A peak power gotcha as SIM800L.
Reads MIFARE Classic / Ultralight cards. NOT 5V tolerant — use only with 3.3V boards or level-shift. Picked for door locks, attendance systems, badge scanners.
Reads/writes ISO14443A/B and FeliCa, plus card emulation and peer-to-peer NFC. Picked over MFRC522 when you need iPhone NFC interaction (only some iOS apps support reader mode).
10 Mbps only — slow, no hardware TCP offload. EtherCard or UIP libraries on Arduino. Don't pick for new designs; W5500 is faster and the same price.
100 Mbps with hardware TCP/IP offload — runs 8 sockets in parallel without the MCU. Stock Ethernet library on Arduino works directly. Picked over ENC28J60 for any new Ethernet design.

constraints.rationale: A 3.3V MCU GPIO is not 5V tolerant on most modern parts (ESP32, RP2040, SAMD21/51, nRF52). Driving 5V into a 3.3V input damages the chip — sometimes immediately, more often a slow degradation that shows as random resets weeks later.

Two I2C devices with the same 7-bit address can't coexist on a single bus. Common collision pairs: BMP280/BME280 (0x76/0x77), VL53L0X/VL53L1X/TCS34725/TSL2591 (all 0x29). Resolve with an I2C mux or by toggling XSHUT/ADDR pins at boot.

Stall current of the motor must be below the driver's continuous rating, not its peak. A2.5 A NEMA17 paired with a 1.2 A A4988 (de-rated, no heatsink) skips steps and overheats. Pick TMC2208/DRV8825 for 2 A NEMA17, BTS7960 for high-current DC.

Feather Wings have a 16+12 pin header pattern that matches Feather boards (and Particle Argon/Photon 2). They do NOT fit UNO, MKR, Nano, Pico, QT Py, or M5 form factors. Carriers and boards must share form factor.

Raspberry Pi Pico boards do not run the official Arduino core. They use Earle Philhower's arduino-pico (a third-party core) for Arduino IDE support, plus CircuitPython and MicroPython. Sketches that depend on Arduino-AVR-specific includes (avr/io.h) won't compile.

Board USB rails (UNO, ESP32 DevKit, Feather USB) deliver about 500 mA before brown-outs. Servos, NeoPixel rings, MQ-2 heaters, and 12V solenoids all draw more than that — needs an external supply with a common ground.

Boards with onboard LiPo charging (Feather, MKR, QT Py via add-on, M5 series) skip the external TP4056. UNO/Nano/Mega have no battery support and require an external charger + boost converter, which most makers underspec.

915 MHz is licence-free in the US; in the EU it's 868 MHz; in Asia 433 MHz. A wrong-region LoRa module is illegal to operate and can be seized. Verify region before recommending RFM95 / RFM69 variants.
AT&T shut down 2G in 2017, T-Mobile in 2022, Telstra/Optus/Vodafone AU 2017, Singapore 2017. SIM800L cannot connect on a US/AU/SG SIM today. Pick LTE-M (A7670G/SIM7080) or NB-IoT.

ESP32 awake pulls 80-240 mA. A 1000 mAh LiPo lasts 4-12 hours in always-on mode. Battery projects must use deep-sleep duty cycling (sub-10 microamp idle) or accept the runtime — there's no third option.

goals.prompt: Prefer entities whose voltage matches the chosen board's logic level so no level shifter is required.
Across all selected entities, minimize the number of distinct external power supplies the user has to provide.
When two sensors are equally compatible, prefer the one with an open-source Arduino/CircuitPython library (Adafruit, SparkFun, U8g2). Avoid closed BSEC-style stacks unless explicitly asked.
Prefer sensors with STEMMA QT or Qwiic connectors when the board supports them — solder-free assembly survives more breadboard accidents.
